clusters.title: AFL icon accused of 'petrifying' assault on woman
clusters.neutral_headline: AFL icon accused of 'petrifying' assault on woman

items.title: AFL icon accused of 'petrifying' assault on woman
items.standfirst: Former St Kilda footballer and racism advocate Nicky Winmar is fighting allegations he attacked a woman almost a year ago.
items.content: A woman has alleged AFL great Nicky Winmar dragged her by the hair and smashed her head against a door during a "petrifying" attack.The woman, who cannot be identified, made the accusations at Bendigo Magistrates Court today as Winmar fought four assault charges in a contested hearing.Police allege the former St Kilda star, 60, attacked the woman at Cohuna in northern Victoria on May 14, 2025.READ MORE: Regional community in mourning after newborn found dead at campsite She told the court Winmar unexpectedly became angry that evening and started yelling at her."Just complete ranting and screaming at me - none of it made sense," she told the court."We hadn't been arguing or anything like that. I couldn't work out what was going on. I was in absolute shock."The woman alleged Winmar grabbed her arm and twisted it hard before pulling her by the ponytail and dragging her into another room.She said Winmar then pushed her against a wall, before yelling and spitting in her face."He turned off the light and wouldn't let me out," the woman told the court."He physically overpowered me so I couldn't get out. He knew I was scared."The woman told the court he eventually let go of her and she ran to grab her mobile phone but he took it off her.READ MORE: Drug dog sits next to suitcase at Sydney Airport, sparking major find He only passed the phone back after she said she wanted to call a friend instead of the police, the court was told.The woman said she spoke to her friend before passing the phone back to Winmar and running from the room.He allegedly chased after her and grabbed her head before bashing it repeatedly into a wooden door."I was getting dizzy, it was incredibly painful," she told the court.The woman said she managed to break free and lock herself into a room, where she called Triple Zero."I was petrified he was going to do more things to me," the woman said in her evidence."I was actually fearful for my life."The Triple Zero call was played to the court alongside body-worn camera footage from a police officer who attended the scene.A photo of the woman's arm was also shown, depicting bruising above her right elbow.READ MORE: Matthew was slugged $2640 in 'confusing' water bill bungle Under questioning from Winmar's barrister, Dermot Dann KC, the woman accepted she did not tell the Triple Zero call taker nor the first officer what exactly happened.She did not disclose the allegations Winmar had dragged her or that he spat in her face."I didn't do that deliberately," the woman told the court."I didn't want to relive it again because I had been sitting in that room absolutely broken by what had just happened."The contested hearing will continue tomorrow.Winmar became the first Aboriginal footballer to play 200 games in the AFL, finishing his career with 230 games at St Kilda and 21 for the Western Bulldogs.He fought back against racism in his career, including standing in front of an abusive Collingwood crowd in 1993, lifting his jumper and proudly pointing at his skin.Winmar is also co-leading a landmark racism class action against the AFL in the Victorian Supreme Court.Support is available from the National Sexual Assault, Domestic and Family Violence Counselling Service at 1800RESPECT (1800 737 732).

items.title: Court hears allegations Nicky Winmar 'bashed' woman's head against door
items.standfirst: A Victorian court has heard allegations former AFL great Nicky Winmar "bashed" a woman's head against a wooden door with such force it cracked during an alleged assault in May 2025.
items.content: A Victorian court has heard allegations former AFL great Nicky Winmar "bashed" a woman's head against a wooden door with such force it cracked during an alleged assault in May 2025.

clusters.title: Australia and Japan sign agreements on defence, energy and critical minerals – video
clusters.neutral_headline: Australia and Japan sign agreements on energy, defence and critical minerals
bullet_summary: Reported by Guardian Australia, ABC News Australia

items.title: Australia and Japan sign agreements on defence, energy and critical minerals – video
items.standfirst: The prime minister, Anthony Albanese, and his Japanese counterpart, Sanae Takaichi, have announced a new agreement to elevate the 'special strategic partnership' between the two countries following talks in Parliament House in Canberra. The high-level agreement includes statements on economic security, energy trade, critical minerals and defence and security. 'For Australians, it will mean we are less vulnerable to global shocks like we are seeing right now because of conflict in the Middle East,' Albanese says Continue reading...
items.content: The prime minister, Anthony Albanese, and his Japanese counterpart, Sanae Takaichi, have announced a new agreement to elevate the 'special strategic partnership' between the two countries following talks in Parliament House in Canberra. The high-level agreement includes statements on economic security, energy trade, critical minerals and defence and security. 'For Australians, it will mean we are less vulnerable to global shocks like we are seeing right now because of conflict in the Middle East,' Albanese says Continue reading...
